1: Psalm 129 is a communal lament over historical persecutions, using an agricultural metaphor for oppression. The opening call to recital echoes Ps 124:1: "Again and again they have attacked me since my earliest days (me'ne'urai) — let Israel say this now" — the liturgical invitation to corporate recitation of historical suffering. "Since my earliest days" (since my youth) — the corporate "Israel" speaks as an individual: the personification of the nation as a person who has suffered from youth, from the very beginning of its existence as a people. Egypt, Canaan, Assyria, Babylon — Israel's enemies since youth. The pilgrim community saying this psalm on the ascent to Jerusalem was re-living the entire history of national persecution as a shared identity narrative.
2: "Again and again they have attacked me since my earliest days, and yet they have never overcome me (lo yakhlu li)" — the repetition of v1a in v2a is both staircase parallelism and liturgical emphasis: the congregation says it twice, and the second time the conclusion is added: "and yet they have never overcome me." The confession of suffering and the confession of survival are inseparable: Israel's whole history of persecution is also the history of never-conquered survival. The "never overcome" is the theological claim at the center of the psalm: multiple empires have attacked, but none has managed to destroy the covenant community. This is not triumphalism (the suffering is real) but covenant confidence (the suffering does not have the last word).
3: "They plowed across my back like farmers cutting their furrows (harashim harashim al-gavi); they made them long and deep (he'eriku lema'anitam)" — the plowing metaphor for oppression is one of the Psalter's most arresting images. The agricultural image is precise: plowing opens the earth by cutting long, deep furrows; the oxen pull the plow across the field with sustained, repeated force. To plow across a person's back with the same force and sustained repetition is to describe sustained, methodical torture or oppression — not a single blow but a systematic, agricultural-scale assault on the body/community. The "long furrows" suggest the extended duration of the oppression: not a brief attack but a sustained agricultural operation of destruction.
4: "But the LORD is righteous (YHWH tzaddik) — he has cut through the ropes (qitzetz abot) the wicked used to bind me" — the ropes (avot, braided cords, harness ropes) that attach the plow to the oxen: YHWH has cut through the ropes that connected the plowing instrument to the power behind it, severing the mechanism of oppression. Isa 10:27 uses the same image of the Assyrian yoke being broken: "the yoke will be broken because of the anointing." Tzaddik (righteous, just) — YHWH's righteousness is what motivates the cutting of the ropes: because YHWH upholds the right order, oppression cannot continue indefinitely. The divine righteousness is not an abstract attribute but an active force that cuts through the structures of oppression.
5: "May every one who hates Zion (sone Tziyyon) be put to shame and driven back" — the imprecatory petition for the reversal of Zion's enemies. "Hates Zion" — in the biblical framework, Zion is not merely a city but the covenant dwelling of YHWH; to hate Zion is to hate what YHWH has chosen and where YHWH dwells. The petition for shame (boshet) and retreat (nasog ahor) is a request for the same reversal-of-fortune that the psalm has described in Israel's history: those who attacked Israel from her youth have been driven back repeatedly; may this continue. The imprecation is not vengeance (personal retaliation) but justice-petition: may the enemies of the covenant community receive the consequences of opposing the divine choice.
6: "May they be like grass growing on a flat rooftop (hasot gag), which withers before it even has a chance to grow" — the rooftop grass image: flat roofs in ancient Israel were typically beaten earth or plaster, and any vegetation that sprouted in the cracks had no significant depth of soil. Whatever grass grew on a rooftop was rootless, thin, and unable to survive the drying sun without any water retention. It grew quickly (easily visible green shoots in the first spring rain) and withered almost immediately when the rains stopped — "before it has a chance to grow" is literally "before it has been plucked up" (implying it never reaches harvestable height). The image is of impressive initial appearance that amounts to nothing at all.
7: "No reaper ever fills his hands with it, and no one gathers it into sheaves" — the agricultural futility image completed: the rooftop grass cannot be harvested because it produces nothing harvestable. The reaper who fills his hands with grain from a good field (Ruth 2:3: Boaz's reapers filling their hands with cut grain) finds nothing to fill his hands on the rooftop. The sheave-binder who follows behind the reaper has nothing to bundle. The wicked's prosperity, like rooftop grass, looks alive but produces no harvest — no lasting fruit, no accumulated legacy, no genuine yield from the brief display of apparent vitality.
8: "And no one passing by will say, 'The LORD's blessing be on you! We pronounce blessing over you in the name of the LORD.'" — the harvest blessing was a formal social exchange between the landowner and the reapers in his field (Ruth 2:4: "Boaz greeted the harvesters and said, 'The LORD be with you!' and they responded, 'The LORD bless you!'" — this is the exchange described in Ps 129:8). The blessing between landowner and reaper was the covenant social seal on a successful harvest. The wicked who produce rooftop-grass prosperity receive no such exchange: no one blesses them in the name of YHWH because there is no harvest to bless, no fruit to celebrate, no covenant community that shares in what they have produced.
